title: Nepali Noodle Sadeko

ingredients.name: noodles (wheat noodles or rice noodles)
visual_checks: Use flat or round wheat noodles for the most authentic texture. For a gluten-free version, use rice noodles and skip the soy sauce.
ingredients.notes: Use flat or round wheat noodles for the most authentic texture. For a gluten-free version, use rice noodles and skip the soy sauce.

subs.name: Rice vermicelli (cooked per packet) for a gluten-free version — reduce the soy sauce and add ½ tsp extra lemon juice.

ingredients.name: gingelly (sesame) oil
visual_checks: Toss with noodles immediately after draining to prevent sticking and add a toasty aroma.
ingredients.notes: Toss with noodles immediately after draining to prevent sticking and add a toasty aroma.

subs.name: 1 tsp toasted coconut oil gives a different but pleasant aroma; plain oil is a neutral fallback.
1 tsp dark tamarind water plus a pinch of salt provides a similar sour-salty flavour without soy or gluten.

ingredients.name: lime or lemon juice
visual_checks: Freshly squeezed; the bright sourness is essential.
ingredients.notes: Freshly squeezed; the bright sourness is essential.

ingredients.name: green chillies, finely sliced
visual_checks: Use less for a milder salad.
ingredients.notes: Use less for a milder salad.

ingredients.name: spring onions, thinly sliced (white and green parts)
sesame seeds, lightly toasted
visual_checks: Toast in a dry pan over medium heat until golden — do not walk away, they burn quickly.
ingredients.notes: Toast in a dry pan over medium heat until golden — do not walk away, they burn quickly.

ingredients.name: roasted peanuts, coarsely crushed
visual_checks: Adds crunch and a pleasant nuttiness; omit for a peanut-free version.
ingredients.notes: Adds crunch and a pleasant nuttiness; omit for a peanut-free version.

steps.text: Boil the noodles in a large pot of salted water according to the packet instructions — usually 3–5 minutes for wheat noodles. Drain immediately and rinse under cold running water to stop the cooking and prevent clumping. Toss with the sesame oil while still warm.

Toast the sesame seeds in a dry frying pan over medium heat, stirring constantly, for 2 minutes until golden and fragrant. Tip onto a plate to cool — they will continue to toast from residual heat if left in the pan.

In a large mixing bowl, whisk together the soy sauce, lemon juice, red chilli powder, and grated ginger. Taste the dressing — it should be sour, salty, and hot. Adjust any of these three elements before adding the noodles.

Add the cooled, sesame-oiled noodles to the dressing bowl. Add the sliced spring onion, green chillies, and fresh coriander. Using tongs or two forks, toss everything together thoroughly until the noodles are evenly coated in the dark, fragrant dressing.

Taste and adjust salt, sourness, or chilli. Let the salad rest for 3 minutes so the noodles absorb the dressing. Scatter the toasted sesame seeds and crushed peanuts (if using) over the top and serve immediately at room temperature.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: noodles → Rice vermicelli (cooked per packet) for a gluten-free version — reduce the soy sauce and add ½ tsp extra lemon juice.; soy-sauce → 1 tsp dark tamarind water plus a pinch of salt provides a similar sour-salty flavour without soy or gluten.; gingelly-oil → 1 tsp toasted coconut oil gives a different but pleasant aroma; plain oil is a neutral fallback.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Use any hakka noodles, Maggi-style noodles (cooked plain without the masala sachet), or rice vermicelli available in Indian grocery stores. Sesame oil (nallennai) is sold at South Indian grocery stores and online. The tossed salad technique is identical to a chaat — think of it as a spiced noodle chaat. Ready-made roasted peanuts in shells work; just break and use.
